title: Worldliness, Humility, and Dependence on God
subtitle: The source of quarrels, friendship with the world, and submitting to God

sections.header: Verses 1-10 - "Submit to God, Resist the Devil"

heb.paragraph: Your passions are at war within you. James traces conflict to internal desires.
You adulteresses! Friendship with the world is enmity with God.
Humble yourselves before the Lord, and he will exalt you.

refs.note: Do not love the world or the things in the world.
God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.
The LORD mocks the proud but gives favor to the humble.

notes.note: Quarrels come from passions at war within. You do not have because you do not ask. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

Calvin sees James exposing the root of social discord: disordered desire. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

God is jealous for His people. The solution to worldliness is repentance. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: You murder is startling. James may use murder hyperbolically. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

moo.source: Douglas Moo, Pillar James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Moo sees this as the letter climax. The problem is worldliness. Moo's exegesis situates James within the broader wisdom tradition, noting how the author draws on Proverbs, Sirach, and Jesus's own teaching to construct a practical theology of faithful living. The epistle's imperatives are grounded not in legalism but in the transformative reality of the 'implanted word' (1:21).

mccartney.source: Dan McCartney, BECNT James — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: McCartney connects this to the double-minded person of 1:8. McCartney connects this passage to the OT prophetic tradition, reading James as a continuation of Israel's wisdom and prophetic heritage adapted for the messianic community. The epistle's social ethic is not novel but draws deeply on the Torah's vision of justice, generosity, and covenant solidarity.

hist.historical: James writes to 'the twelve tribes in the Dispersion' — Jewish Christians scattered throughout the Roman Empire after persecution in Jerusalem (Acts 8:1; 11:19). The letter reflects the earliest stage of Christianity when believers still gathered in synagogues (2:2, Greek synagōgē). James, the brother of Jesus, led the Jerusalem church (Acts 15; Gal 2:9) until his martyrdom in AD 62. His letter addresses practical concerns: economic disparity between rich and poor, the relationship between faith and works, and the power of the tongue. The wisdom tradition of Judaism pervades the letter, echoing Proverbs and Jesus' Sermon on the Mount. The agricultural imagery (1:11; 3:12; 5:7) and references to early and late rains reflect the Palestinian context.
hist.context: James diagnoses the source of community conflict: unchecked desires. The solution is radical: submit to God, resist the devil, draw near to God.

sections.header: Verses 11-17 - "Do Not Judge; Do Not Boast"

heb.paragraph: Do not speak evil against one another. Slandering a brother is setting oneself above the law.
What is your life? You are a mist that appears and then vanishes.
You boast in your arrogance. All such boasting is evil.

refs.note: Judge not, that you be not judged.
The parable of the rich fool.

Jesus' teaching on earthly versus heavenly treasure — the same materialism and misplaced priorities that James confronts in those who plan commercial ventures without acknowledging God's sovereignty over the future.
Do not boast about tomorrow, for you do not know what a day may bring — the wisdom-tradition foundation for James' rebuke of presumptuous planning that ignores human finitude and divine sovereignty.

notes.note: Speaking evil against a brother is judging the law. There is one Lawgiver and Judge. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

moo.source: Douglas Moo, Pillar James — Scholarly Paraphrase

mccartney.source: Dan McCartney, BECNT James — Scholarly Paraphrase

hist.context: James addresses two sins: judging brothers and presumptuous planning. Life is a vapor. Right planning acknowledges if the Lord wills.

themes.note: Sovereignty dominates: God opposes the proud, gives grace to the humble.

rows.label: vv. 1-6
rows.text: Source of quarrels; friendship with the world

lit.note: Chapter 4 exposes worldliness and arrogance. Both require submission to God.

discourse.title: James 4 — Worldliness, Humility, and Submission to God
discourse.thesis: Conflicts arise from disordered desires. Submit to God, resist the devil, humble yourselves. Do not judge; do not boast about tomorrow.

nodes.label: Source of conflicts (4:1-3)
nodes.text: What causes quarrels? Is it not passions at war within you? You desire and do not have, so you murder. You covet and cannot obtain, so you fight. You do not have because you do not ask. You ask wrongly, to spend on your passions.

nodes.label: Friendship with world (4:4-6)
nodes.text: Adulterous people! Friendship with the world is enmity with God. Whoever wishes to be a friend of the world makes himself an enemy of God. Scripture says God yearns jealously over the spirit he made to dwell in us, but gives more grace.

nodes.label: Submit and resist (4:7-10)
nodes.text: Submit to God. Resist the devil and he will flee. Draw near to God and he will draw near to you. Cleanse your hands, purify your hearts, you double-minded. Humble yourselves before the Lord and he will exalt you.

nodes.label: Do not judge (4:11-12)
nodes.text: Do not speak evil against one another. If you judge your brother, you judge the law. There is only one lawgiver and judge; who are you to judge your neighbor?

nodes.label: Boasting about tomorrow (4:13-17)
nodes.text: Come now, you who say 'Today or tomorrow we will go to such a city and make a profit.' You do not know what tomorrow brings. You are a mist. Instead say, 'If the Lord wills.' All such boasting is evil. Whoever knows the right thing and fails to do it, for him it is sin.

discourse.note: The 'double-minded' language echoes 1:8. James returns to themes cyclically rather than linearly.

coaching.tip: 'What causes quarrels and what causes fights among you? Is it not this, that your passions are at war within you?' (v. 1). Conflict traces to disordered desires. James psychologizes communal strife: external battles reflect internal wars. Want more, fight more. The cure isn't better conflict resolution but transformed wanting.

'You do not know what tomorrow will bring... you ought to say, If the Lord wills, we will live and do this or that' (vv. 14-15). Planning as if you control the future is arrogant. 'Lord willing' isn't superstition but theological realism. Human plans are provisional; divine sovereignty is ultimate. Humility includes calendar humility.

prayer_prompt: As James traces quarrels to desires battling within, consider asking God to reveal the unmet want underneath your most recent conflict — and whether you brought it to Him or tried to seize it yourself.
